title: Play a 'love letter' to city's migrants
content:
A festive stage play charting the experience of migrants in Oxford is a "love letter" to earlier generations of immigrants to the city, according to its producer.

Before the Millenium is told through the eyes of two Pakistani immigrants arriving in Oxford in 1999.

Written by Karim Khan, it follows two young women - Zoya and Iqra - working in Woolworths on Cowley Road as the millennium approaches, before a ghostly visit from the future intervenes.

It deals with issues faced by immigrants at the time, including homesickness, the challenges of settling in a completely new country and learning a new language.

Producer Jessie Anand said it particularly reflected the experience of her own parents' generation.

"It tries to understand better what they went through, what they lost, what they gained," she said.

The show showed South Asian and Muslim audiences that "their stories matter too".

Ms Anand added the show was a form of "resistance", using "theatre as a tool to change people's minds in a way that they weren't expecting".

Director Adam Karim said: "It provides a Pakistani-Muslim lens on the holiday season, which we don't have in popular culture.

"If we don't tell our own stories, someone else will. The news cycle is filled with politicians telling falsehoods about us.

"Generally, we're so much more nuanced, complex, and diverse. The absence of us telling our own stories is allowing that narrative to take hold."

Gurjot Dhaliwal, who plays Zoya, said more funding and commissions for Asian-led narratives was needed in order to capture authentic voices.

"You can tell when it's [a play] been written by a white man.

"It is so jarring. You've made me into this two-dimensional character… that doesn't exist. It's not my mum, it's not my aunty, it's not my sisters. It's a character that doesn't exist in real life."

The 26-year-old, who is also an up-and-coming writer, notes that when cast and crew reflect the voices on the stage, authentic characters are the result.

"Zoya felt like home to me, because of that connection to my mum… when they come to a completely new country, learn a whole different language.

"But before that, they were just girls. They were young women – who had dreams, aspirations, hopes.

"But they couldn't go back home. They just had to make it work," she said.

Before the Millennium is on at The Old Fire Station in Oxford until 21 December.
